deep_analysis.executive_intro:
This FTSE 100 screen is broadly neutral: 69 names are on hold, versus only eight actionable ideas (four strong buys and four buys) and 17 avoids, with six stocks flagged for insufficient data. Conviction is thin even among the leaders — every top candidate is a new signal (one week at rating) with conviction scores between 33% and 45% — so the actionable set is small and not yet battle-tested by persistence. Top candidates cluster in cyclical, commodity-linked sectors: two Basic Materials names (Rio Tinto, Endeavour Mining), one Energy (Shell), and two Consumer Cyclical exposures (Barratt Redrow, Howden Joinery), suggesting the screen is favouring cheap, cash-generative cyclicals rather than defensive compounders. One important data limitation: several models report missing inputs (Graham Net-Net lacks NCAV for all five; Earnings Quality flags missing operating cash flow), and Piotroski F-Scores are weak across the board (3/9 to 6/9), so balance-sheet and earnings-quality conclusions should be treated as incomplete until manual verification.

**Rio Tinto Group (RIO.L)** — Flagged Strong Buy on 10/22 models (composite 74%, sector-relative 77%), passing all five factor families (cheapness, quality, dividend, GARP, risk). Key passes include Graham Enterprising, Quality Value, Buffett Quality, High Dividend Yield, Magic Formula, Acquirer's Multiple, Composite Value, and Financial Health, supported by P/E 14.7, P/B 2.4, yield 4.5%, ROE 16.4%, and D/E 35%. Failures include Lynch PEG (negative earnings growth at -5.6%), FCF Yield (3.2% vs 5% threshold), and Piotroski F-Score (3/9). Sector concentration risk is meaningful alongside Endeavour Mining — two of five top picks are Basic Materials miners. **Verdict: accumulate** — broad multi-model cheapness and quality support a position, but commodity cyclicality and weak fundamental momentum scores warrant staged entry per the neutral timing signal (RSI 35).

**Endeavour Mining plc (EDV.L)** — The strongest quantitative profile among top candidates: Strong Buy on 11/22 models (composite 74%, highest model count), passing all five families including FCF Yield (17.8%), Lynch PEG (PEG 0.13, growth 105.7%), Quality Value, Buffett Quality, Economic Moat, Magic Formula, Acquirer's Multiple, and Financial Health. Metrics: P/E 13.7, P/B 3.5, yield 2.9%, ROE 31.1%. Graham Enterprising failed on P/B >= 3; Piotroski F-Score 6/9. Shares Basic Materials concentration with Rio Tinto. **Verdict: accumulate** — the deepest model support and exceptional FCF yield/ROE combination justify priority, though gold-mining cyclicality and below-200-day MA positioning (-10.2%) argue for limit-order discipline.

**Shell plc (SHEL.L)** — Strong Buy on 9/22 models (composite 62%, sector-relative 81% — best sector-relative score in the group), passing cheapness, dividend, GARP, and risk families. Passes include Graham Enterprising, FCF Yield (9.2%), Lynch PEG (PEG 0.48, growth 26.6%), Neff PEGY, Dividend Growth, Magic Formula, Acquirer's Multiple, Dreman Contrarian, and Financial Health, with P/E 12.7, P/B 1.3, yield 3.8%, ROE 10.7%, D/E 43%. Notably failed Quality Value, Buffett Quality, and Economic Moat (ROE below 12%, thin margins at 7.0%); Piotroski F-Score 3/9. **Verdict: accumulate** — compelling valuation and cash generation at sector-leading relative scores, tempered by absent quality-family passes and energy-cycle exposure.

**Barratt Redrow plc (BTRW.L)** — Strong Buy on 8/22 models (composite 67%, sector-relative 73%), passing cheapness, dividend, GARP, and risk — but not quality. Passes centre on deep asset cheapness: Schloss Low P/B (P/B 0.53, D/E 3%), Deep Value (EV/EBITDA 6.9), Lynch PEG, Neff PEGY, High Dividend Yield (6.1%), Dividend Growth, Composite Value, and Financial Health. Key metrics show P/E 19.2, P/B 0.5, yield 6.1%, but ROE is only 2.8% and FCF is negative (-£77.2m). Failed FCF Yield (-1.9%), Quality Value, Buffett Quality, Economic Moat, and Piotroski F-Score (4/9). Pairs with Howden Joinery for Consumer Cyclical concentration (two of five picks). **Verdict: watchlist** — extreme book-value discount and dividend screen appeal are offset by negative free cash flow, sub-par ROE, and UK housing-cycle sensitivity; confirm earnings quality before committing capital.

**Howden Joinery Group Plc (HWDN.L)** — Rated Buy (not Strong Buy) on 7/22 models (composite 60%, sector-relative 63%), passing all five families including FCF Yield (5.1%), Neff PEGY (PEGY 0.06), Quality Value, Buffett Quality, Dividend Growth, Magic Formula, and Financial Health. Metrics: P/E 16.0, P/B 3.6, yield 2.8%, ROE 23.1%. Failed Lynch PEG (PEG 1.74), Deep Value, Acquirer's Multiple, Composite Value, and Piotroski F-Score (5/9); timing notes a downtrend (price below 50- and 200-day MAs). **Verdict: watchlist** — solid quality-and-GARP profile but weaker composite score, no trade plan in the payload, and technical downtrend suggest waiting for trend confirmation or a better entry.

The screen does not capture several qualitative risks that matter for these names:

**Commodity and macro cyclicality** — Rio Tinto, Endeavour Mining, and Shell are all exposed to global commodity price cycles, capital-spending swings, and geopolitical supply disruptions. Rio shows negative earnings growth (-5.6%) in the model data; mining and energy earnings can deteriorate rapidly when prices turn.

**UK housing and consumer cyclicality** — Barratt Redrow faces structural and cyclical UK residential construction risk (mortgage affordability, planning policy, post-merger integration following the Barratt/Redrow combination). Howden Joinery is tied to RMI (repair, maintenance, improvement) spending and small-trade installer demand, which softens in downturns.

**Earnings quality and cash conversion** — Barratt Redrow's negative FCF (-£77.2m) alongside a 6.1% quoted yield is a balance-sheet red flag the quant screen only partially surfaces (FCF Yield model failed; Earnings Quality flagged weak free-cash conversion and missing OCF). Shell's FCF/NI ratio of 0.88 and Rio's weak Piotroski score (3/9) suggest operating-cash-flow durability deserves manual review.

**Quality-model failures on Shell** — Despite a Strong Buy signal, Shell failed Buffett Quality, Quality Value, and Economic Moat (ROE 10.7%, margins 7.0%), pointing to an energy major valued on cheapness and cash yield rather than franchise quality — relevant for long-term energy-transition capex and stranded-asset debate.

**Regulatory and ESG** — Mining (Rio, Endeavour) carries jurisdiction, permitting, and tailings-dam regulatory risk; Shell faces climate-policy, litigation, and transition-investment pressures. None of this appears in the JSON.

**Governance and pensions** — The payload contains no research verdicts (all `research_verdict`, `research_risk_level`, and `research_rationale` fields are null) and no pension-deficit or governance data. These cannot be assessed from this screen alone.

**Data artefacts** — Several model reason strings show implausibly scaled dividend yields (e.g. 609% for BTRW, 445% for RIO in raw model output vs 6.1% and 4.5% in `key_metrics`), suggesting feed-normalisation issues that could affect dividend-model reliability.

**Names warranting deeper fundamental research (up to 3):**
1. **Endeavour Mining (EDV.L)** — highest model pass rate (11/22) and FCF yield (17.8%); verify sustainability of 105.7% earnings growth and jurisdictional risk across West African operations.
2. **Shell (SHEL.L)** — best sector-relative composite (81%) with strong FCF yield; assess energy-transition capex, reserve replacement, and whether quality-model failures reflect structural margin compression.
3. **Barratt Redrow (BTRW.L)** — deepest book-value discount (P/B 0.5) but negative FCF and 2.8% ROE; priority research on dividend safety, land-bank writedowns, and post-merger synergy delivery before any position.

research.name: Barratt Redrow plc
research.executive_summary: Barratt Redrow is the UK's largest listed housebuilder following the Barratt–Redrow merger, and the quantitative screen flags it as a Strong Buy on cheapness (P/B 0.5), dividend yield (6.1%), GARP, and balance-sheet risk metrics, with a sector-relative composite score of 73%. The valuation hook is a sub-book, high-yield cyclical trading well below its 200-day moving average, but earnings and margins have collapsed from 2022–23 peaks and free cash flow has nearly dried up. The central debate is whether this is a classic asset-backed value entry ahead of a housing/policy recovery, or a value trap given sector-wide derating, integration risk, and a potential £4bn+ competition lawsuit. Conviction on the screen signal is low (33%) and timing is neutral, so the case rests on balance-sheet optionality rather than near-term earnings momentum.
